The tier "Probable" is appropriately justified by the derivative nature of the index (not the original GRO register) and the indeterminate quality of the census birth information, while noting that the original GRO register would upgrade the conclusion to "Proved." entry.question: When and where was Robert Raymond McKee (son of Robert McKee and Margaret Pollock, resident Newry, County Down, Ireland in 1911, died 1958 Chicago) born?
entry.rationale: This is the direct GPS question matching the project objective. L8K8-W12 has no birth fact in the tree. His parents married June 1890 in Newry, County Down; he resided there in 1911 and immigrated to North America in 1923. Irish civil registration (births registered from 1864) and Ireland census records (1901, 1911) are the primary repositories; church registers for the Newry area are also available on FamilySearch. A birth circa 1890–1910 in County Down is well within surviving record coverage.

entry.rationale: Three immigration/border-crossing records already sourced in the tree (St. Albans Canadian Border Crossings 9YN8-JHB, Canada Immigration Records Q36D-6VF, US Border Crossings 9YNC-RDY) have not yet been read for birth-date and birthplace assertions. St. Albans manifests and Canadian Form 30A routinely recorded age, birth year, and country of origin; some list the exact birthplace. Reading these first extracts the most immediately available birth evidence without a new search.

Ireland, Civil Registration, 1845-1913 (collection 2659409) is the primary authoritative source for the subject's birth. Irish civil registration began in 1864; births were recorded by district registrar in the Newry Superintendent Registrar's District, County Down. A civil birth entry would give the exact date, place (townland), and father's name — direct evidence for both the date and place objective. Search for 'Robert McKee' born Newry district, County Down, circa 1890-1910.

Ireland Census 1901 (collection 1626180) is fully indexed and survived intact. Robert McKee (LT1G-QP2) and Margaret Pollock married 1890 in Newry; Robert Raymond McKee should appear as a child in their household circa age 0-11. The census return gives each member's age, relationship, county of birth, and religion — indirect but corroborating evidence for birth year and birthplace county.

Ireland Census 1911 (collection 2854327) confirms Robert Raymond McKee was in Newry in 1911 (tree fact). The original census schedule gives his exact stated age (and thus birth year), county of birth, and religion — important corroborating evidence. The 1911 census also uniquely records how many years each couple had been married and how many children born/living, helping profile the family.

entry.jurisdiction: Stockton, San Joaquin, California, United States
entry.rationale: Robert R. McKee registered for the WWII draft on 15 Feb 1942 in Stockton, CA (tree fact). Series III draft registration cards recorded the registrant's exact birth date and birthplace — often the most precise US-held record of a foreign-born person's birth. FamilySearch holds the WWII Draft Registration Cards collection. This corroborates or refines what the immigration records say.

Ireland, Catholic Parish Registers, 1740-1900 (collection 2820100) includes baptism registers for the Newry area. The Archdiocese of Chicago cemetery record (Q36D-ZKP) placing Robert in a Catholic cemetery suggests a Catholic background. A baptism entry would give date of birth or baptism, father's name, mother's maiden name, and sometimes witnesses — direct evidence for date and locality. Search Newry RC parish baptisms for Robert McKee circa 1890-1910. Note: this collection ends 1900, so births after 1900 may only appear in unindexed volumes or on Ancestry (Ireland, Catholic Parish Registers, 1655-1915, collection 61039).

IrishGenealogy.ie (https://civilrecords.irishgenealogy.ie) provides free online access to civil birth registration images 1864-1923 — the same General Register Office records behind the FamilySearch index but with direct image access and sometimes more flexible searching. Searching here is a cross-check on the FamilySearch civil registration result (pli_002) and may surface records the FamilySearch index missed (transcription errors in surname 'McKee' vs 'Mc Kee' vs 'MacKee' are common).

Resolve the birth-year conflict for subject L8K8-W12 (Robert Raymond McKee), question q_001.

**The conflict:**
- a_002 (Civil Registration Index, src_001, derivative, primary/witness): birth Q1 1904, Banbridge, County Down — most authoritative; birth registered near event; registrar's index of original GRO registration
- a_005 (Ireland Census 1911, src_002, original, indeterminate/household_member): born 1904 — corroborates civil reg; independent original source; 7 years after birth
- a_009 (St. Albans border crossing 1923, src_004, derivative, secondary/self): birth 1900 — conflicts; same informant as a_011 and a_013
- a_011 (Canada immigration 1923, src_005, derivative, secondary/self): birth 1903 — conflicts; same informant as a_009 and a_013
- a_013 (US border crossing 1923, src_006, derivative, secondary/self): birth Nov 1900 — conflicts; same informant as a_009 and a_011

**Evidence independence analysis (from assertion-classification):**
- a_009, a_011, a_013 all derive from the SAME informant (the subject himself self-reporting in 1923) — they form ONE evidence unit for birth year, not three independent claims
- The "opposing" side is therefore: 1 evidence unit (self-reporting 1900/1903/1900) vs. 2 independent sources (civil registration + 1911 census) both giving 1904

**Known context for weighing evidence:**
- A person cannot have primary information about their own birth year (classification was changed to secondary during assertion-classification)
- Immigrants commonly misreported age at border crossings and on immigration forms (motivated by perceived advantages — appearing older to avoid dependency status, confusion with documents, etc.)
- The subject was not yet 20 in 1923 if born 1904; misreporting birth year as 1900 would make him appear 23 instead of 19
- The civil registration record is primary (parent-informant, close in time), derivative (quarterly index), and the most proximate to the birth event
- The 1911 census is an original source; subject's absence from 1901 census further corroborates 1904 birth
- No conflict exists on birthplace: all sources consistently give Banbridge, Ireland (civil reg: Banbridge, County Down; Canada immigration: Banbridge, Ireland; US border: Banbridge) — only the St. Albans record gives the broader "Ireland"

Please formally register this conflict and resolve it, determining which birth year assertion(s) to accept.

entry.description: Robert Raymond McKee birth year: civil registration index and 1911 census both state 1904; three 1923 immigration records (same informant) state 1900, 1903, and November 1900.

fields.independence_analysis: The competing evidence falls into two groups. Group 1 (supporting 1904): the civil registration index entry (a_002) and the 1911 Ireland census (a_005) are treated as substantially independent — they derive from different government bureaucracies (General Register Office vs. Registrar General/Census), different years (1904 registration vs. 1911 census), and likely different informants (the registering parent in 1904 vs. an unknown household respondent in 1911). A correlation exists: the same parent who registered the birth might also have answered the 1911 census enumerator; however, because the records are from different administrative systems at different times, they do not share a single derivation chain and constitute two evidence units for practical purposes. The subject's absence from the April 1901 census (a_008) provides a third, fully independent corroboration: a different original source from a different year, showing the subject did not yet exist in that household. Group 2 (supporting 1900/1903): a_009 (St. Albans border crossing, birth year 1900), a_011 (Canada Form 30A, birth year 1903), and a_013 (US border crossing, birth year November 1900) all derive from the SAME informant — Robert Raymond McKee himself, self-reporting in 1923. These three assertions form a single evidence unit. Their internal inconsistency (three different values: 1900, 1903, November 1900) within the same year from the same person further undermines the unit's credibility. The effective comparison is therefore: two independent government sources corroborated by a third (1901 absence) versus one self-reporting evidence unit with internal inconsistency.
fields.weighing_analysis: Three factors are decisive. First, informant proximity: the civil registration's parent-informant witnessed the birth and registered it within 42 days, providing contemporaneous first-hand reporting with no evident motive to misreport the date. The subject self-reporting in 1923 could not have first-hand knowledge of his own birth and was reporting 19 years after the event — at minimum from memory of what he had been told, at maximum from deliberate misstatement. Second, internal consistency of the self-reports: the subject gave three different birth years in a single year (1900, 1903, and November 1900), making none individually reliable — an informant with accurate knowledge would give consistent values across documents filed within months of each other. Third, the 1901 census absence independently falsifies the 1900 birth claim: Robert McKee (LT1G-QP2) and Margaret McKee (LJK3-9QP) are positively identified in the 1901 Newry census, and no child named Robert Raymond appears in their household — confirming the subject was not yet born by April 1901, which rules out any birth year of 1900 or earlier. Together these factors strongly favor 1904.
fields.resolution_rationale:
The dispute concerns Robert Raymond McKee's birth year: the Ireland Civil Registration index and the 1911 Ireland census agree on 1904, while three 1923 immigration records from the same self-reporting informant give 1900, 1903, and November 1900. Resolving the correct year matters because it affects every age-based document in the subject's record trail and the interpretation of his 1923 immigration.

The civil registration entry (a_002) was compiled from the original GRO birth registration for Robert Raymond M'kee, Banbridge Superintendent Registrar's District, Q1 (January–March) 1904. A parent — almost certainly the father — registered the birth within the statutory 42-day period, reporting directly to the district registrar. The 1911 Ireland census (a_005) independently records the same subject as born in 1904, in the verified household of his parents at Newry, Banbridge, County Down. The subject's absence from the April 1901 Ireland census (a_008), in the same positively-identified household, independently eliminates any birth year of 1900 or 1901. Against these, the three 1923 immigration records form a single evidence unit: all were self-reported by the subject, who could not have first-hand knowledge of his own birth date and gave three inconsistent values (1900, 1903, November 1900) within a single year.

The civil registration and census are accepted. The 1904 birth year is the best-supported answer, corroborated by three independent lines of evidence.

The immigration misreporting reflects a well-documented pattern: young men traveling alone in the 1920s frequently stated an older age at immigration inspection to appear economically self-sufficient and avoid scrutiny as minors or dependents. Robert, if born 1904, was 18–19 in 1923; stating birth year 1900 made him appear 23. The internal inconsistency across his three self-reports (1900, 1903, and November 1900) shows he was not reporting from a fixed document — the varying values are characteristic of an improvised or misremembered figure, not a genuine recollection of birth date. This pattern of 'age inflation by young immigrants' is the historically grounded explanation for the discrepancy.

entry.narrative_markdown:
## The Birth of Robert Raymond McKee: Q1 1904, Banbridge, County Down, Ireland

**Research Question:** When and where was Robert Raymond McKee born?

**Conclusion (Probable):** The preponderance of evidence strongly indicates that Robert Raymond McKee — son of Robert McKee (a spirit merchant) and Margaret Pollock McKee of Newry, County Down — was born in the first quarter of 1904 (January through March) in the Banbridge Superintendent Registrar's District, County Down, Ireland.

### Civil Registration Index — Primary Evidence

The *Ireland, Civil Registration Indexes, 1845–1958*, on FamilySearch (collection 1408347), contains a quarterly birth registration entry for "Robert Raymond M'kee," registered in Q1 (January–March) 1904 in the Banbridge district, County Down, Ireland (record ARK: ark:/61903/1:1:FB2F-WZ8).¹ This index is a derivative source compiled from the original General Register Office (GRO) birth registers; the underlying original would supply the exact birth date and townland. The informant at civil registration was the child's parent — a person with firsthand knowledge of the birth event — making the information primary. The entry's distinctive middle name "Raymond," combined with the Banbridge registration district consistent with two of three immigration records, uniquely identifies it as belonging to the subject.

### Ireland Census 1911 — Corroborating Original Source

The 1911 Ireland Census schedule for the household of Robert McKee at Newry, Banbridge, County Down (FamilySearch ARK: ark:/61903/1:1:QYXF-J52M) records "Robert Raymond McKee," age 7, born County Down, religion Unitarian, occupation Scholar.² This is an original source. The enumerator collected household information in April 1911; an age of 7 at that date is consistent with a birth in early 1904. The informant is unknown (a pre-1940 census custom), so the birth-year information is classified as indeterminate rather than primary, but the record independently corroborates both the birth year (1904) and the birthplace county (County Down) established by the civil registration index. The household head is identified as the subject's father, Robert McKee (FamilySearch tree person LT1G-QP2).

### Ireland Census 1901 — Negative Evidence

A search of the 1901 Ireland Census for the same Robert McKee household at Newry, Banbridge, County Down (collection 1626180) found the father but no child named Robert Raymond McKee.³ This absence is meaningful: the 1901 census was taken in March–April 1901, more than three years before the Q1 1904 birth registered in the civil index. A child not yet born in April 1901 would not appear in the census. The absence is fully consistent with — and provides independent negative corroboration of — the 1904 birth date.

### Conflicting Evidence and Its Resolution

Three 1923 immigration records record the subject's birth year differently: a St. Albans Canadian border crossing lists 1900 (ARK: ark:/61903/1:1:QK3T-VKK3),⁴ a Canadian immigration manifest lists 1903 (ARK: ark:/61903/1:1:68FM-F3TF),⁵ and a U.S. border crossing from Canada lists November 1900 (ARK: ark:/61903/1:1:FSZ6-QNG).⁶ These three records form a single evidence unit: the informant in each case was Robert Raymond McKee himself, self-reporting to immigration officials approximately nineteen years after his birth. A person cannot have firsthand knowledge of their own birth, so these self-reports are classified as secondary information. Furthermore, the three records are internally inconsistent with one another — they give three different birth years — which significantly undermines their collective reliability for birth year. The immigration records do consistently name Banbridge as birthplace, independently corroborating the civil registration district.

The birth year discrepancy is explained by the well-documented pattern of age misreporting by immigrants in the early 1920s. Young men often inflated their apparent age when crossing borders to appear more employable, or simply did not know their exact birth year. McKee appears to have consistently reported a birth year of approximately 1900 (with one record giving 1903), rounding down from the actual 1904 — a two-to-four-year discrepancy consistent with casual misreporting rather than deliberate fraud. The civil registration index, resting on a parent-informant present at the birth, is substantially more reliable for the birth year. Conflict c_001 is resolved in favor of the civil registration.

A WWII draft registration (FamilySearch ARK: ark:/61903/1:1:QGF4-CZQH), indexed as born 29 February 1904 with birthplace "Milford, Massachusetts," was examined but found unreliable: the image linked to this indexed record (3Q9M-CSLK-RBG8) shows a Series III "Old Man's Registration" card for a different Robert Raymond McKee born 26 October 1892. The image is mislinked in FamilySearch and was not used as evidence.

### Tier Rationale

This conclusion is assessed at **Probable** rather than Proved because the primary civil registration finding rests on a derivative quarterly index, not the original GRO birth register. The corroborating 1911 census carries indeterminate (not primary) information quality. Two independent evidence units agree on the birth year 1904 and on Banbridge, County Down as the location; the conflict with immigration records is fully resolved; and the research is declared reasonably exhaustive. Consulting the original GRO birth register via IrishGenealogy.ie (https://civilrecords.irishgenealogy.ie) would be required to advance this conclusion to Proved and to establish the exact birth date within Q1 1904.

---
¹ "Ireland, Civil Registration Indexes, 1845–1958," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:FB2F-WZ8 : accessed 9 July 2026), entry for Robert Raymond M'kee, birth registration Q1 1904, Banbridge, County Down.
² "Ireland, Census, 1911," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:QYXF-J52M : accessed 9 July 2026), Robert Raymond McKee in household of Robert McKee, Newry, Banbridge, County Down.
³ "Ireland, Census, 1901," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org : accessed 9 July 2026), household of Robert McKee, Newry, Banbridge, County Down — subject Robert Raymond McKee absent.
⁴ "Vermont, Franklin, St. Albans, Canadian Border Crossings, 1895–1954," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:QK3T-VKK3 : accessed 9 July 2026), entry for Robert R McKee, 1923.
⁵ "Canada, Immigration Records, 1919–1924," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:68FM-F3TF : accessed 9 July 2026), entry for Robert R. McKee, 1923.
⁶ "United States, Border Crossings from Canada to United States, 1895–1956," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:FSZ6-QNG : accessed 9 July 2026), entry for Robert R McKee, 1923.

# Mentor review: proof-critique on ps_001

## What you've done well
Your independence analysis in c_001 is exactly right: you recognized that a_009, a_011, and a_013 all trace to the same informant (the subject himself, self-reporting in 1923) and treated them as one evidence unit rather than three independent corroborations — this is Standard 46 done correctly. Your resolution_rationale for c_001 is a model of Standard 48's four-part structure: you state the problem, lay out both evidentiary groups, explain why the civil registration/census pairing is more reliable (informant proximity, internal consistency), and then explain *why* the immigration records diverge (the well-documented age-inflation pattern among young 1920s immigrants). You also deserve credit for catching the mislinked WWII draft card image (log_010) and excluding it rather than letting a convenient 29 Feb 1904 birthdate slip into the proof uncritically — that's Standard 43 in action.

## Worth considering
Two things would strengthen this before the case is fully closed.

First, your identity rationale and narrative both lean on the claim that the Banbridge registration district is 'consistent with' the subject's 1911 Newry residence. Historically, Newry and Banbridge were separate, adjacent registration districts in County Down — not one within the other. The identity match is not threatened (the distinctive middle name 'Raymond' plus the matching parents in the 1911 census do that work on their own), but the geographic claim as written is imprecise. The more accurate story is that the family was living in or near Banbridge at the time of the 1904 birth and removed to Newry by 1911.

Second, you have already named the document that would move this to Proved: the original GRO birth register on IrishGenealogy.ie. It is free, targeted, and the gating item is already identified. The overturn-risk-is-low argument is sound for Probable now, but this is a low-cost, high-value lookup for whenever you are ready to push toward Proved.

## Overall
This is careful, honest work. The tier call (Probable) is correctly calibrated — not inflated, not under-tiered given the derivative-index/quarter-only precision limits you named yourself. That self-aware tiering is exactly what Standard 43 asks for.
